metadata.paper_title: Balancing Needs, Efficiency, and Functionality in the Provision of Modeling Software: A Perspective of the NIH WinSAAM Project

system_context.system_description: A Windows-based migration of the SAAM (Simulation, Analysis, and Modeling) and Consam software, designed to provide kinetic modeling tools for nutritional research.
system_context.business_goals: To achieve a blend which isolates critical functionality, flexible control, and user support while meeting the needs of the broadest collection of serious users.

evaluation_setup.evaluation_goal: To discuss the issues impacting the migration of SAAM to the Windows environment and outline the steps taken to ensure its feasibility.

architectures.name: WinSAAM
architectures.description: An open, component-based architecture incorporating a legacy processing kernel (SAAM) with a modern Windows interface using third-party modules and dynamic linking libraries (DLLs).

key_components.responsibilities: Supervisory role; Terminal input, line-oriented text editor, CLI-style processing control.
Assemble input components, edit project specification, manage projects.
Line-oriented text editor, graphic text management.
Tabular-oriented input, processing, output, and formatting.
Coordinate user files, facilitate access, check integrity.
Plotting results, configuring plots, chart-based communication.
Processing heart of WinSAAM; system solving and iterative parameter adjustment.
Provide salient models from important application areas (e.g., lipid metabolism).

assumptions: Users skilled in particular domains will be able to be registered as library domain contributors.
Former Consam users will be immediately able to run WinSAAM in a productive way.

scenarios.scenario_text: Incorporate the total functionality of SAAM and Consam into WinSAAM.
Preserve the operational flow of Consam (support a 'terminal' window).
Integrate the 'look and feel' of Windows products (file management, clipboard access, widgets).
Build on a third-party component basis (incorporate editors, spreadsheets, graphics tools).
Design for an open style of operation (mode-less but protected operational flow).
Design for extensibility (modeling libraries and extended functionality via DLLs).
Accommodate communication with other software (data transportation to pre/post-processing software).
Accommodation of an efficient help facility.
Ensure that WinSAAM is consistent, reliable, and efficient.
Distribute application activities amongst the user community (incorporate domain libraries from experts).
Preservation of the SAAM kernel; architecture, and organization.

scenario_classification.rationale: Requires WinSAAM to function as a superset, implying addition of functionality beyond the original.
Kernel incorporated in its current form; restructuring was not justified.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Incorporation of third-party software tools (editors, spreadsheets, graphics).
issues_identified: Savings offered by adopting this strategy.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Integration of new processing algorithms via dynamic linking libraries.
issues_identified: Enhance capabilities without risking corrupting the kernel code.

architectural_strengths: Access to vast array of software add-ins (third-party components).
Competitive edge in assuring overall robustness by preserving the SAAM kernel.
High returns for effort by focusing on maintenance and enhancement rather than restructuring.
architectural_weaknesses: Mode-less software requires greater development effort.

architecture_comparison.rationale: The architecture successfully blends critical functionality with flexible control, allowing for incremental prototyping and extensibility via DLLs and third-party components.

recommendations: Use incremental prototyping paradigm for development.
Postpone restructuring of the SAAM kernel.
Distribute application activities (domain libraries) amongst the user community.

missing_information: Explicit SAAM Method classification (Direct/Indirect) for all scenarios.
inference_notes: The paper describes the 'WinSAAM' software project and its design objectives, but does not explicitly apply the 'Scenario-Based Architecture Analysis Method' (SAAM) as a formal evaluation methodology.
Classifications (Direct/Indirect) were inferred based on whether the text described using existing components (Direct) or developing new features/integrations (Indirect).
